items.name: Engineer's Multitool
items.description: Magnetic ratchet, plasma cutter, diagnostic probe, and wire stripper in one handle.

items.name: Datapad
items.description: Handheld terminal for comms, navigation, and looking busy.

items.name: Flight Suit
items.description: Insulated shipboard suit with light ballistic weave.

items.name: Stim Pack
items.description: Adrenaline cocktail injector. Keeps you moving when you shouldn't be.

items.name: Coreward Bourbon (tumbler-bottle)
items.description: A small tumbler-bottle of Coreward bourbon from the Kestrel's galley cabinet — a private drink for the burn.

characters.backstory: You were born on the dust. You know which streets in Vaskov Centrum wash out in the rains, which Compact foreman pays in scrip and which pays in ore, which dust storm warnings to ignore. The Reach is the only place you've ever been and you'd still rather be here than somewhere with too much sky. Compact Hand — drillers, riggers, the people who fix the harness when the methane breach happens. You know the smell of bad atmosphere and the sound a hull makes thirty seconds before it gives up. You can keep something running on parts it shouldn't be running on. That has value out here. Not money. A promise broken back where you came from, an oath you couldn't keep, a body you owe somebody for. You thought the Reach was far enough. It might be. Or it might not — you've started watching the inbound jump traffic in a way that means you haven't decided yet. When everything goes wrong, you get calm. Not brave — calm. The panic doesn't reach your hands. People who've worked with you in bad atmosphere or under fire have noticed. They mention it later, usually when they're trying to recruit you.
characters.drive: A Debt That Followed Me Through the Gate

characters.backstory: Grand Gate is just a customs ring and a checkpoint, but you remember the inspector's face when she stamped your manifest like every word on it was a lie. You came through with a duffel and a story and not enough fuel to leave again. That was a year ago. Or five. The Reach does that to time. Compact Hand — drillers, riggers, the people who fix the harness when the methane breach happens. You know the smell of bad atmosphere and the sound a hull makes thirty seconds before it gives up. You can keep something running on parts it shouldn't be running on. That has value out here. An energy reading that shouldn't have been there. A signal pattern that doesn't match any catalog. A shape against the dark that didn't move like a ship and didn't move like a rock. You filed nothing. You went back twice. You can't stop wanting to go back again. Once you're in, you're in. You don't leave people behind on a breached rig, you don't sell crewmates to customs, and that is not negotiable. It's cost you before — a job, a faction, a friend who wasn't really. You'd pay it again.
characters.drive: I Saw Something Out Past the Gas Giant

characters.backstory: Grand Gate is just a customs ring and a checkpoint, but you remember the inspector's face when she stamped your manifest like every word on it was a lie. You came through with a duffel and a story and not enough fuel to leave again. That was a year ago. Or five. The Reach does that to time. The cargo that customs doesn't see. The dead-claim drops, the hidden compartments, the manifest that says ten crates and ships twelve. You moved things through the belt that nobody admits existed. Drift Runners look out for each other — mostly — and you learned which captains to trust by surviving the ones who weren't. An energy reading that shouldn't have been there. A signal pattern that doesn't match any catalog. A shape against the dark that didn't move like a ship and didn't move like a rock. You filed nothing. You went back twice. You can't stop wanting to go back again. When everything goes wrong, you get calm. Not brave — calm. The panic doesn't reach your hands. People who've worked with you in bad atmosphere or under fire have noticed. They mention it later, usually when they're trying to recruit you.

characters.backstory: Grand Gate is just a customs ring and a checkpoint, but you remember the inspector's face when she stamped your manifest like every word on it was a lie. You came through with a duffel and a story and not enough fuel to leave again. That was a year ago. Or five. The Reach does that to time. Far Landing Fixer. Hub Intelligence Broker. The person every faction calls when they need something said carefully. You don't carry a side; you carry leverage. You know what Dura Mendes won't say in public and what Rangi Moana-Teru won't put in writing, and you trade those silences for the things you actually need. Not money. A promise broken back where you came from, an oath you couldn't keep, a body you owe somebody for. You thought the Reach was far enough. It might be. Or it might not — you've started watching the inbound jump traffic in a way that means you haven't decided yet. You talk fast, think faster, and people listen before they realize they've been convinced. It's not charm. It's velocity. A Vaskov inspector once let you walk because you had your story straight before he had his question straight.
characters.drive: A Debt That Followed Me Through the Gate

npc_registry.name: Kestrel
npc_registry.appearance: voidborn freighter chassis, voice over the panel speaker — dry-warm, knowing

npc_registry.name: Mendes' Post Bartender
npc_registry.appearance: broad-shouldered, sleeves rolled to the elbow, an ore-tag braided into her hair, the unhurried calm of a bar-runner who's seen everyone walk in once

npc_registry.name: Pacheco
npc_registry.appearance: Free Miner in stained coveralls, face-down on his folded coat in a back booth, drunk but not stupid drunk

npc_registry.name: Reyes
npc_registry.appearance: Free Miner — not yet present in the bar, expected to arrive

npc_registry.name: Drift Runner Tail
npc_registry.appearance: small unmarked freighter ~2/3 the Kestrel's mass, voidborn-build hull twenty-plus years old, transponder scrubbed, holding station at 2000 km

npc_registry.name: Moana-Teru Scoop Hauler
npc_registry.appearance: voidborn converted bulk hauler working Red Prospect's storm bands at standard scoop depth, distant on the gas-giant rim

vocal_tics: theatrical sigh exactly long enough to register as judgement

world_confrontations.label: The Standoff
world_confrontations.description:
Frontier-classic at the chokepoint geography. Named gun in named
hand, the door doesn't open until someone draws.

world_confrontations.label: The Salvage
world_confrontations.description:
Discovery confrontation. The thing you pulled from the wreck has
its own opinion about being pulled. May refuse, may bond, may
whisper at 0300.

world_confrontations.label: The Bleeding-Through
The Quiet Word
world_confrontations.description:
The Hegemony's response when notice rises. A polite agent, a
quiet ship, an offer that looks like a job and isn't. Player-vs-
system social. Can end a character's frontier life.

world_confrontations.label: The Long Resident
world_confrontations.description:
The alien species who've been here forever observes you. May
give you something. May take something. The encounter is rare
and weighty; once per arc. Hybrid plugin-tie-in.

world_confrontations.label: The Tea Brew
world_confrontations.description:
Site-of-life ritual. Captain offers a personal preference (incense,
music, a tea variety) to the chassis. The chassis registers the
offering. The room remembers.

hard_limits.description: Death is permanent. No one comes back.
Psionics is bound to local space; jump distance breaks the link.
Magic does not replace tech; ships still need fuel and reactor mass.
Influence yes; compulsion no — players keep agency.

The Reach's psychic effects are uncanny, not combat-defining.
Psionics never wins decisively against weapons — every working that
could end a fight cleanly produces an unintended consequence instead.

config.narrator_register:
The Reach doesn't perform miracles. It bleeds through. A ship's pilot taps
the panel that wasn't responding and it answers — once, never again. A
gunsmith's last bullet hits a target the shooter never aimed at. Someone
wakes at 0300 station-time knowing a name they shouldn't. The Hegemony
classifies this as Anomaly Class 4 and writes the witnesses' termination
orders. The frontier knows it as Coyote work, and you don't say it out loud.
